info.structure: Part 5: Chapter 1, Section 6. Exploring the cosmic correlations (Adhidevata) of the Sāman, detailing the earth, fire, sky, stars, and the golden person within the Sun.
info.format: Devanagari, IAST, English translation, and philosophical commentaries.

sections.title: The Cosmic Interdependence of Sāman and the Golden Person in the Sun

verses.devanagari: इयमेवर्ग् अग्निः साम तदेतदेतस्यामृच्यध्यूढं साम तस्मादृच्यध्यूढं साम गीयते। इयमेव साग्निरमस्तत्साम ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: iyamevarg agniḥ sāma tadetadetasyāmarcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma tasmādrcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma gīyate | iyameva sāgniramastatsāma || 1 ||
verses.english_translation: The Earth is the Rik, and Fire is the Sāman. This Sāman rests completely upon that Rik. Therefore, the Sāman chant is sung as resting upon the Rik. The Earth is 'Sā' and Fire is 'Ama', and that makes 'Sāman'.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara explains that this section begins a systematic meditation designed to merge the components of the vocal chant with cosmic realities. A Sāman melody cannot exist without the structural container of a Rik verse. Allegorically, the moving, transformative element of Fire (*Agni*) rests upon the stable foundation of the Earth (*Pṛthivī*). Shankara breaks down the word etymologically: *Sā* represents the foundational earth, and *Ama* represents the animating fire. This reveals that the very structure of scriptural song mirrors the physical balance of nature.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja reads this as a manifestation of the structural harmony built into creation by the Supreme Ruler. The Earth and Fire are not random elements; they are functional, interdependent limbs of the cosmic body of God. By visualizing the ritual chant as identical to these cosmic forces, the worshiper learns to see the physical planet as an altar sustained by divine music.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya points out that *Sā* and *Ama* refer to the internal presiding deities of the earth and fire, respectively. These deities are structurally dependent upon one another because they are both animated by different, coordinated forms of Lord Viṣṇu. The meditation teaches the soul to recognize the singular, organizing power that coordinates independent cosmic forces.

verses.devanagari: अन्तरिक्षमेवर्ग् वायुः साम तदेतदेतस्यामृच्यध्यूढं साम तस्मादृच्यध्यूढं साम गीयते। अन्तरिक्षमेव सा वायुरमस्तत्साम ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: antarikṣamevarg vāyuḥ sāma tadetadetasyāmarcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma tasmādrcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma gīyate | antarikṣameva sā vāyuramastatsāma || 2 ||
verses.english_translation: The Mid-region (space) is the Rik, and the Air is the Sāman. This Sāman rests completely upon that Rik. Therefore, the Sāman chant is sung as resting upon the Rik. The Mid-region is 'Sā' and the Air is 'Ama', and that makes 'Sāman'.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the ascent of meditation into the next cosmic layer. The vast, intermediate space (*Antarikṣa*) acts as the passive, open matrix (the Rik), while the active, moving wind (*Vāyu*) fills it and flows through it (the Sāman). The linguistic pairing remains consistent: *Sā* is the space, *Ama* is the air. By superimposing these qualities, the text helps the meditator expand their mind from the solid earth into the fluid, atmospheric dynamics of the universe.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school notes that the relationship between space and wind shows how the invisible forces of the Lord operate. The wind cannot blow without the container of space, yet space is made dynamically alive by the movement of the wind. This perfect, natural cooperative system reflects the intentional design of the Supreme Controller.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the deity of Air is Mukhya Prāṇa in his atmospheric form, which rests within the space controlled by the deity of the sky. The meditation directs the seeker to look past the physical air currents to see the absolute dependence of the life-giving atmospheric gods upon the Supreme Lord.

verses.devanagari: द्यौरेवर्ग् आदित्यः साम तदेतदेतस्यामृच्यध्यूढं साम तस्मादृच्यध्यूढं साम गीयते। द्यौरेव सादित्योऽमस्तत्साम ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: dyaurevarg ādityaḥ sāma tadetadetasyāmarcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma tasmādrcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma gīyate | dyaureva sādityo'mastatsāma || 3 ||
verses.english_translation: The Heaven is the Rik, and the Sun is the Sāman. This Sāman rests completely upon that Rik. Therefore, the Sāman chant is sung as resting upon the Rik. The Heaven is 'Sā' and the Sun is 'Ama', and that makes 'Sāman'.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Here the meditation reaches the highest visible cosmic plane. The distant, luminous sky (*Dyauḥ*) is the foundation, and the blazing Sun (*Āditya*) is the dynamic light resting within it. Shankara emphasizes that this upward movement of thought—from earth to mid-space to heaven—is deliberately designed to stretch the human intellect, preparing it to grasp the all-pervading nature of Consciousness.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja points out that the Sun in the heavens represents the grandest physical manifestation of divine splendor available to human eyes. Linking the Sun with the Sāman chant ensures that the priest performs the ritual with an expansive heart, fully aware that the liturgy connects directly with the highest cosmic realities.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, this pairing highlights the relationship between the solar deity and the heavenly space. The independent energy of Viṣṇu operates through the Sun to illuminate the upper worlds, and this meditation honors that specific solar dynamic within the cosmic hierarchy.

verses.devanagari: नक्षत्राण्येवर्क् चन्द्रमाः साम तदेतदेतस्यामृच्यध्यूढं साम तस्मादृच्यध्यूढं साम गीयते। नक्षत्राण्येव सा चन्द्रमा अमस्तत्साम ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: nakṣatrāṇyevark candramāḥ sāma tadetadetasyāmarcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma tasmādrcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma gīyate | nakṣatrāṇyeva sā candramā amastatsāma || 4 ||
verses.english_translation: The Stars are the Rik, and the Moon is the Sāman. This Sāman rests completely upon that Rik. Therefore, the Sāman chant is sung as resting upon the Rik. The Stars are 'Sā' and the Moon is 'Ama', and that makes 'Sāman'.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara handles the nocturnal sky. The scattered, fixed constellations (*Nakṣatras*) form the canvas, while the moving Moon (*Candramāḥ*) acts as the sovereign entity that coordinates and passes through them. This poetic cosmic identity reinforces the theme that every aspect of the observable universe is balanced by a passive foundation and an active agent, all pointing to the single underlying reality of Brahman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school notes that the beautifully ordered movement of the moon through the stars is an expression of the cosmic aesthetic (*saundarya*) of the Lord. The meditation turns astronomy into an act of deep contemplation, helping the soul see divine symmetry in the night sky.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva identifies the Moon and the stars as home to specific administrative deities who govern time, tides, and human minds. Meditating on this relationship emphasizes that all celestial movements are bound by cosmic laws established by the Supreme Person.

verses.devanagari: अथ यदेतदादित्यस्य शुक्लं भाः सैवर्ग् अथ यन्नीलं परः कृष्णं तत्साम तदेतदेतस्यामृच्यध्यूढं साम तस्मादृच्यध्यूढं साम गीयते ॥ ५ ॥
verses.iast: atha yadetadādityasya śuklaṃ bhāḥ saivarg atha yannīlaṃ paraḥ kṛṣṇaṃ tatsāma tadetadetasyāmarcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma tasmādrcyadhyūḍhaṃ sāma gīyate || 5 ||
verses.english_translation: Now, that white radiance of the Sun is the Rik, and that which is intensely blue or deep black within it is the Sāman. This Sāman rests completely upon that Rik. Therefore, the Sāman chant is sung as resting upon the Rik.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara dives deep into the visual anatomy of solar light. The blinding, outer white glow (*śuklaṃ bhāḥ*) of the Sun is identified as the Rik. If you gaze deeply past that white light, there is a mysterious, intensely dark, ultra-black core (*nīlaṃ paraḥ kṛṣṇam*), which is identified as the Sāman. This dark, hidden core resting inside the brilliant white radiance is a profound metaphor for the unmanifested, ungraspable reality that sits right at the center of the most visible cosmic objects.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja highlights this verse as a critical scriptural source for describing the beautiful form of the Lord. The deep, intense blackness within the sun is not a literal absence of light, but the mesmerizing, cloud-dark hue (*Megha-śyāma*) of the Supreme Person who dwells at the heart of the solar system, radiating beauty from the inside out.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that the white light and the deep black core represent two different, superimposed energies of Lord Viṣṇu. The white light handles the material illumination of the physical worlds, while the inner, dark radiance represents the concentrated presence of pure spiritual power governing the inner functions of the cosmos.

verses.devanagari: अथ यदेतदादित्यस्य शुक्लं भाः सैव सा अथ यन्नीलं परः कृष्णं तदमस्तत्साम। अथ य एषोऽन्तरादित्ये हिरण्मयः पुरुषो दृश्यते हिरण्यश्मश्रुर्हिरण्यकेश आप्रणखात्सर्व एव सुवर्णः ॥ ६ ॥
verses.iast: atha yadetadādityasya śuklaṃ bhāḥ saiva sā atha yannīlaṃ paraḥ kṛṣṇaṃ tadamastatsāma | atha ya eṣo'ntarāditye hiraṇmayaḥ puruṣo dṛśyate hiraṇyaśmaśrurhiraṇyakeśa āpraṇakhātsarva eva suvarṇaḥ || 6 ||
verses.english_translation: The white light of the sun is 'Sā' and the deep black core is 'Ama', making 'Sāman'. Now, this Golden Person who is seen deep within the Sun, with a golden beard, golden hair, and who is completely golden right to the very tips of his nails—

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara provides an essential warning regarding the word 'Golden' (*Hiraṇmayaḥ*). It does not mean the Supreme Lord is made of physical, metallic gold, which would make Him finite and subject to melting. Rather, 'golden' is a metaphor for pure, self-luminous light (*jyotirmaya*). This 'Person' (*Puruṣa*) is the cosmic soul, the localized expression of Brahman animating the solar sphere. He is described with a beard and hair to give the human mind a concrete, luminous focal point for advanced meditation.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: This is a foundational text for Ramanuja's theology. The description proves that the Supreme Brahman is not a featureless void, but possesses an eternal, non-material, incredibly beautiful divine form (*Divya-maṅgala-vigraha*). This form is made of pure spiritual substance (*Suddha-sattva*), shining like refined gold, and is visualized by advanced yogis through single-pointed devotion.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva asserts that this Golden Person is Lord Narayana Himself, residing inside the sun to direct the universe. The form is completely spiritual and eternal. The description of being golden down to the fingernails emphasizes that every single limb of the divine form is independent, perfect, and completely free from any material limitations or decay.

verses.devanagari: तस्य यथा कप्यासं पुण्डरीकमेवमक्षिणी तस्योदिति नाम स एष सर्वेभ्यः पाप्मभ्य उदितः उदेति ह वै सर्वेभ्यः पाप्मभ्यो य एवं वेद ॥ ७ ॥
verses.iast: tasya yathā kapyāsaṃ puṇḍarīkamevamakṣiṇī tasyoditi nāma sa eṣa sarvebhyaḥ pāpmabhya uditaḥ udeti ha vai sarvebhyaḥ pāpmābhyo ya evaṃ veda || 7 ||
verses.english_translation: His eyes are like a lotus flower that has bloomed under the morning sun. His name is 'Ut' (Risen), for he has risen above and is completely free from all evil. He who knows this truth rises completely above all evil.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara handles the famous and controversial phrase *kapyāsaṃ puṇḍarīkam*. He interprets it linguistically to mean a lotus flower sitting on its stalk, fully opened by the warmth of the sun. The eyes of this cosmic person are wide, bright, and radiating pure awareness. His secret name is *Ut* because He is completely detached from (*udita*) the laws of cause, effect, and sin (*pāpma*). By realizing our ultimate identity with this cosmic person, we automatically rise above our own psychological baggage and past karma.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Ramanuja famously refined the commentary on *kapyāsaṃ* to rescue it from crude translations, proving it means a lotus deeply rooted in rich waters and opened beautifully by the sun's rays. The lotus-eyes of the Lord convey deep, unconditional compassion (*Karuṇā*). His name *Ut* shows His absolute purity; He is untouched by material flaws, and by meditating on Him, the devotee is pulled out of the mire of material suffering.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that *Ut* is an exclusive name for the Independent Lord, indicating that He stands infinitely above all created things and flaws. The comparison of His eyes to a freshly bloomed lotus illustrates His gentle, life-giving gaze toward His creation. The seeker who understands the absolute purity of the Lord is cleansed of their sins through divine grace.

verses.devanagari: तस्य र्क् च साम च गेष्णौ तस्मादुद्गीथस्तस्मात्त्वेवोद्गातैतस्य हि गाता स एष ये चामुष्मात्पराञ्चो लोकास्तेषां चेष्टे देवकामानां च ॥ ८ ॥
verses.iast: tasya rk ca sāma ca geṣṇau tasmādudgīthastasmāttvevodgātaitanya hi gātā sa eṣa ye cāmuṣmātparāñco lokāsteṣāṃ ceṣṭe devakāmānāṃ ca || ८ ||
verses.english_translation: The Rik and the Sāman are his two joints or singers; therefore, he is the Udgītha. For this reason, the priest who sings his praise is called the Udgātṛ. This Golden Person rules over all the worlds that lie beyond the Sun, and over all the desires of the gods.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara notes that the Rik and Sāman are called His joints or singers (*geṣṇau*) because the entire vocal liturgy is powered by His cosmic energy. The verse establishes His macrocosmic sovereignty: He rules over everything above the solar sphere (*amutra*) and satisfies the desires of the gods. In Advaita, this cosmic ruler (*Iśvara*) is the personal aspect of Brahman, managing the vast networks of cosmic cause and effect with absolute intelligence.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The cosmic majesty of the Lord is displayed here. He is the master of all higher spiritual and material realms (*Parāñco Lokāḥ*). The Vedic liturgies are simply musical praises directed toward His cosmic body. Ramanuja teaches that the Lord's supreme rulership means He possesses the absolute capacity to grant any pure desire, whether it belongs to a cosmic deity or a humble human devotee.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva emphasizes the words 'he rules' (*īṣṭe*). This confirms that Lord Narayana exercises complete, unshared sovereignty over the entire universe above the solar realm. The gods themselves are dependent on Him for their positions and desires, proving that no cosmic force possesses independent power outside the supreme will of Viṣṇu.
